subject: Physics
solution:
Isobars are atoms of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons (mass number $A$), but different atomic numbers ($Z$).
In the given options:
(1) $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$: Both have the same mass number $A = 3$. Since they have different atomic numbers ($Z=1$ and $Z=2$), they are isobars.
(2) $_1^2\text{H}$ and $_1^3\text{H}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=1$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
(3) $_{80}^{198}\text{Hg}$ and $_{79}^{197}\text{Au}$: These have different mass numbers ($198$ and $197$).
(4) $_{92}^{236}\text{U}$ and $_{92}^{238}\text{U}$: These have the same atomic number ($Z=92$) but different mass numbers. They are isotopes.
Thus, the pair $_1^3\text{H}$ and $_2^3\text{He}$ are isobars.

subject: Physics
solution:
The nuclear reaction is given by:

$2 X^{3} + Y^{4} \rightarrow Z^{10}$

The total binding energy of the reactants is the sum of the binding energies of the two nuclei of mass number $3$ and the nucleus of mass number $4$.

Binding energy of reactants $= 2 \times (3 \times 5.6) + (4 \times 7.4)$

$= 33.6 + 29.6 = 63.2$ MeV

The total binding energy of the product nucleus of mass number $10$ is:

Binding energy of product $= 10 \times 6.1 = 61.0$ MeV

The change in mass energy $\Delta M c^2$ is equal to the difference in the total binding energies of the reactants and the product.

$\Delta M c^2 = |63.2 - 61.0| = 2.2$ MeV

Answer: $2.2$

subject: Physics
solution:
For the nucleus $^{209}_{83}Bi$, the number of protons is $Z = 83$ and the number of neutrons is $N = 209 - 83 = 126$.

Mass of $83$ protons $= 83 \times 1.007825 = 83.649475 \, u$

Mass of $126$ neutrons $= 126 \times 1.008665 = 127.091790 \, u$

Total mass of the nucleons $= 83.649475 + 127.091790 = 210.741265 \, u$

The mass defect $\Delta m$ is given by:

$\Delta m = \text{Total mass of nucleons} - \text{Mass of nucleus}$

$\Delta m = 210.741265 - 208.980388 = 1.760877 \, u$

The total binding energy ($BE$) is:

$BE = \Delta m \times 931 \, \text{MeV}$

$BE = 1.760877 \times 931 = 1639.376 \, \text{MeV}$

The binding energy per nucleon is:

$\dfrac{BE}{A} = \dfrac{1639.376}{209} \approx 7.84 \, \text{MeV}$

Answer: $7.84$

subject: Physics
solution:
For Rutherford scattering, at the closest approach all kinetic energy converts to electrostatic potential energy.
For alpha particle-nucleus collision:
$K.E. = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{Z_1 Z_2 e^2}{r_{min}}$
Where: $Z_1 = 2$ (alpha), $Z_2 = 79$ (gold), $K.E. = 7.9$ MeV
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.9 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$r_{min} = 2.88 \times 10^{-14}$ m
The estimated nuclear diameter is approximately:
$d = 2 \times r_{min} = 5.76 \times 10^{-14}$ m

subject: Physics
solution:
(A) Electron revolves around nucleus only in those orbits for which the angular momentum is an integral multiple of h2π.

(B) Nuclear force does not follow the inverse square law.

(C) Nuclear forces are spin dependent but charge independent.

(D) Nuclear force is attractive in nature. It is a non central force, meaning it does not depend upon the distance between the particles but rather upon their orientation. Nuclear force is stronger if the spin is parallel; and weak if it is anti parallel.

(E) The negative value (less quantity) of packing fraction indicates that the isotope is stable. Positive value of packing fraction implies unstable (radioactive) nucleus. Also, the stability of the nucleus is inversely proportional to the value of the packing fraction.

n=tT12   ...1

n=15 years5 years= 3

N=N02-n   ...2

Substitute the value of n into equation (2) to obtain the fraction of the remaining substance.

N=N02-3⇒NN0=18

Hence, the amount of the decayed substance can be written as

1-NN0=1-18=78

solution:
The radius of a nucleus, R is related to its atomic number A as:

R=R0A13

where R0 is the nuclear radius of hydrogen.

Thus, the density of a nucleus is given by:

d=μA43πR0A133=3μ4πR03=Constant 

(Here, μ is atomic mass unit)

Hence, the ratio of nuclear density of oxygen to helium nucleus is 1:1

solution:
Given, for ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&A_0=16 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{~A}=12 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5760 \text { years }
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\lambda=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}$
$\lambda=\frac{0.693}{5760}$ per year
Then, from, $\mathrm{t}=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{A}_0}{\mathrm{~A}}$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} \frac{16}{12}
$$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} 1.333
$$
$=\frac{2.303 \times 5760 \times 0.1249}{0.693}$
$=2390.81 \approx 2391$ years

solution:
When a radioactive nucleus emits 1 α-particle, its mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2. During positron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1. Similarly, during electron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1.

So, the value of P=92-2-2+1-1-1

⇒P=92-5

⇒P=87.

solution:
Radioactive decay is a random and spontaneous process, it depends on unbalancing of nucleus. This process is dependent on the law of conservation of charge.

We know that, N=N0e-λt    ...1, Where, N=number of decayed nuclei in the sample at time t and N0=initial number of nuclei.

Hence, total number of undecayed nuclei N0-N

Thus, N0-N=N01-e-λt

This shows that the total number of undecayed nuclei decays exponentially with time. 

Taking log both sides of equation 1

lnN=-λt+lnN0

So, slope =-λ  (reciprocal of mean life time)

Half life time T12=ln2λ

Then, λ×T12=ln2=constant

Hence, (B) and (C) are correct only.

subject: Physics
solution:
In an $\alpha$-decay, the kinetic energy of the emitted $\alpha$-particle is given by the conservation of momentum and energy as:

$K_{\alpha} = \dfrac{A - 4}{A} Q$

where $A$ is the mass number of the parent nucleus.

For substance A ($A = 200$):

$K_{\alpha, A} = \dfrac{200 - 4}{200} Q = \dfrac{196}{200} Q$

For substance B ($A = 212$):

$K_{\alpha, B} = \dfrac{212 - 4}{212} Q = \dfrac{208}{212} Q$

The ratio of the energies of the $\alpha$-rays produced by A and B is:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{196}{200} Q}{\dfrac{208}{212} Q} = \dfrac{196}{200} \times \dfrac{212}{208}$

Simplifying the fractions:

$\dfrac{K_{\alpha, A}}{K_{\alpha, B}} = \dfrac{49}{50} \times \dfrac{53}{52} = \dfrac{2597}{2600}$

Answer: $\dfrac{2597}{2600}$
